7 min read
1 hour ago

--

Okay so this started as a weekend experiment that honestly got out of hand in the best possible way.
The goal was simple — build something useful without spinning up a backend, without learning a new framework, without deploying anything. Just use what was already there.
What was already there? Excel. VBA. And an AI API key.
Spoiler: it worked. And now it is hard to look at a spreadsheet the same way again.
The Frustration That Started All of This
Here is something that happens to everyone who has worked with Excel long enough.
You build a really clean formula. Something like:
=IF(A2="Food", "Essential", "Non-Essential")It works great. You feel good about it. You show it to your team.
Then someone enters “Swiggy” in column A.
The formula returns “Non-Essential.” Which is wrong. So you update it:
=IF(OR(A2="Food", A2="Swiggy"), "Essential", "Non-Essential")Then someone types “Zomato order for team lunch”. Then “Amazon Fresh groceries”. Then “blinkit — vegetables”.
And suddenly the formula is 15 conditions long and still breaking every other week.
This is the ceiling of traditional Excel automation. It is not a skill problem. It is a fundamental limitation:
Excel can only execute rules you already thought of.
VBA macros are more powerful — they can loop through rows, call APIs, format cells, send emails. But they are still deterministic. They still cannot reason. They cannot look at “Swiggy — ₹450 — UPI” and understand on their own that this is a food delivery expense, that it is discretionary, that it is trending upward, and that it is worth flagging.
That kind of reasoning needs something smarter.
So instead of writing more rules, the approach here was to hand the thinking off to an AI — and let Excel just handle the input and output.
What Got Built
No Power BI. No Python server. No Zapier. No new app.
Just Excel, VBA, and an AI API.
Here is what the sheet looks like when a new expense row gets added:
Date | Description | Amount | Payment Method
07-Jul-26 | Swiggy | 450 | UPIThe macro runs. Two API calls are made. The sheet fills itself out:
Category | Necessity | Budget Status | Saving Suggestion | Monthly Trend | Financial Advice | Savings Potential
Food | Discretionary | Within Budget | Cook at home | Increasing | Reduce food delivery expenses | 25%No formula figured that out. No lookup table. No hardcoded rule that says “if Swiggy then Food.”
The AI read the row, understood the context, and reasoned about it — all triggered from inside Excel.

How It Actually Works
The architecture is genuinely simple. That was the most surprising part.
Excel Row → VBA builds a prompt → AI API → VBA parses response → Excel cells filledThree pieces:
1. The Prompt Builder
VBA reads the cell values and constructs a plain English prompt. Something like: “You are a finance assistant. Classify this expense. Return only: Category | Necessity | Budget Status.” Then it passes the row data — date, description, amount, payment method — into that prompt.
2. The API Call
This is the part that surprised most people when they see it. There are no external libraries. No pip install. No npm package. Just this:
Set Http = CreateObject("MSXML2.XMLHTTP")
Http.Open "POST", URL, False
Http.setRequestHeader "Content-Type", "application/json"
Http.send JSONMSXML2.XMLHTTP is a native Windows COM object. It ships with Windows. Every Excel installation already has it. That one object is all that is needed to call any REST API in the world.
3. The Parser
The AI is instructed to return its answer in a pipe-separated format like Food|Discretionary|Within Budget. VBA splits that string and writes each piece into the correct column.
That is the whole thing.
The insight here is not technical complexity — it is a shift in thinking. Instead of writing rules for every possible input, you write instructions to something that can reason. The prompt replaces the formula.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

This Is Just One Example Though
The expense tracker was the proof of concept. But once this pattern clicks — cell input → prompt → AI → cell output — it starts showing up everywhere.
Here are real workflows that can be built the exact same way, inside Excel, without any external tools:
Invoice and Contract Intelligence
Paste raw invoice text or a contract clause into a cell. The AI pulls out:

• Vendor name, amount, due date, payment terms

• Risk flags like “auto-renewal clause detected”

• Suggested next action like “schedule payment reminder 5 days before due date”
No OCR software. No contract management platform. Just Excel.
Email Draft Generator
Sales teams log call notes in Column A. The macro reads each note and writes a personalised follow-up email draft in Column B — ready to copy and send.
Call Notes: "Interested in enterprise plan, concerned about pricing, follow up next week"
↓ AI
Email Draft: "Hi [Name], really enjoyed our conversation today. Pricing is always
an important factor and I'd love to walk you through some options
that might work better for your team..."For small teams that do not need a full CRM — Excel just became the outreach tool.
Sentiment and Feedback Analyser
Paste customer reviews, survey responses, or support tickets into a column. The AI classifies each one:

• Sentiment — Positive, Negative, Neutral

• Topic — Delivery, Product Quality, Support

• Priority — High, Medium, Low

• Suggested response type
What used to need a dedicated NLP pipeline now runs in a spreadsheet the whole team already has open.
HR and Form Processing
An HR team logs interview notes in free text. The macro reads each entry and scores candidates against job criteria — flagging strengths, gaps, and standout responses.
A task that used to take hours of manual reading becomes a two-minute macro run.
⚠ Always keep a human in the loop for decisions like hiring or anything medical. AI assists — it does not decide.
Inventory and Supply Chain Reasoning
Stock sheet has product names, quantities, and sales velocity. The AI reasons through it:

• “This item has 3 days of stock left at current velocity — reorder now”

• “This SKU has not moved in 45 days — consider a promotion”

• “Supplier lead time is 7 days — trigger purchase order today”
Traditional Excel needs a web of formulas and lookup tables for this. AI needs one prompt.
Resume Screener
HR pastes job descriptions in one sheet and candidate summaries in another. The macro loops through and scores each candidate against the role — outputting a match score, key strengths, and gaps.
Competitive Intelligence Tracker
The team logs competitor updates, pricing changes, and news manually. The AI reads each entry and outputs:

• Threat level — High, Medium, Low

• Strategic implication

• Recommended response
The Excel sheet becomes a living competitive intelligence dashboard.
The Pattern Behind All of These
Every single example above follows the same structure:
Messy, ambiguous, human-written input
↓
AI reasons about it
↓
Clean, structured, actionable output back into ExcelThe key idea:
AI does not replace Excel. It fills the gap between what humans write and what Excel can process.
Humans write messy, contextual, inconsistent information. Excel needs clean, structured, categorical data. AI sits in the middle and translates — in real time, inside the tool the whole team already uses every day.

Why Not Just Build a Dedicated App?
Honestly, sometimes you should. If there are hundreds of users, real-time collaboration needs, and a complex custom UI — build the app.
But for most internal business workflows — small teams, departmental processes, operational tasks — a dedicated app means:

• Weeks of development time

• Hosting and maintenance costs

• User adoption friction (“why do I need to learn a new tool?”)

• IT approval processes

• Integration work with existing systems
An AI-augmented Excel sheet means:

• Built in an afternoon

• Zero hosting costs

• Zero adoption friction — everyone already has Excel open

• Runs on any Windows machine

• No IT approval needed for a macro file
For most internal use cases, the ROI comparison is not even close.
Being Honest About the Limitations
This approach is not perfect. Here are the real trade-offs:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

What This Actually Means
For 30 years, Excel automation meant writing more rules. Better formulas. Smarter macros. But always deterministic — always limited to what could be anticipated in advance.
AI changes that entirely.
Now a spreadsheet can handle the inputs nobody anticipated. The descriptions written five different ways. The edge cases the IF statement never covered. The reasoning that used to require a human to look at the data and make a judgment call.
That judgment call can now happen automatically, at scale, inside the tool the entire organisation already runs on.
No new platform. No dedicated app. No data science team.
Just a prompt, an API key, and about 200 lines of VBA.

Try It
The full working code for the expense analyser is here:
Github
To adapt it for a different use case:

• Replace the prompt-builder functions with your own instructions

• Change the column mappings to match your sheet

• Plug in your own AI API key — most providers have a free tier that works fine for testing
The architecture stays identical. Only the prompts change.

One Last Thing
The most powerful AI tool is not always the most sophisticated one.
Sometimes it is the one sitting inside the spreadsheet your team opens every single morning — quietly reading, reasoning, and filling in the answers — while everyone just wonders how the sheet got so smart.
